overview.marketStructure: Mix of Public and Private sector banks. Private banks gaining share (32% deposits from 22% a decade ago). Top 3 private banks (HDFC, ICICI, Axis) control 68% of private sector deposits. Public sector led by SBI with 23% overall market share but losing share to private players.
demandDrivers: India's GDP growth driving credit demand (loans growing at 14-16% annually)
Financial inclusion - 480M+ Jan Dhan accounts expanding banking reach
Digital payments revolution (UPI 12B+ transactions/month) creating fee income
Housing finance boom - home loans growing at 18% CAGR
MSME lending push through government schemes (Mudra, ECLGS)
Working capital demand from manufacturing expansion
Consumer credit growth - personal loans, credit cards expanding

keyRawMaterials.dependency: Retail deposits (CASA - Current Account Savings Account) are cheapest source. Competition intense for low-cost deposits. Corporates, HNIs, retail savers all sources.
keyRawMaterials.supplyRisk: Medium - CASA ratios under pressure (41% to 38% for private banks). Banks competing aggressively. Deposit growth lagging credit growth creating liquidity squeeze.

keyRawMaterials.dependency: Capital adequacy regulated by RBI (minimum 11.5% CAR). Banks raise equity via QIP, rights issues. Tier-2 bonds for additional capital. Basel III norms require higher capital.
keyRawMaterials.supplyRisk: Low - Indian banks well-capitalized (15-18% CAR). Equity markets receptive to bank QIPs. Promoter dilution risk exists for some.

keyRawMaterials.dependency: Core banking systems (Finacle, TCS BaNCS), payment gateways, cybersecurity. Dependent on vendors like Infosys, TCS, Oracle.
keyRawMaterials.supplyRisk: Low - Multiple vendors. However, migration risks exist. Cybersecurity threats increasing.

keyRawMaterials.material: Talent (Relationship Managers, Credit Officers, Tech Teams)
keyRawMaterials.dependency: Domestic talent from IIMs, CA, NIBM. Poaching common among banks. Tech talent from engineering colleges.
keyRawMaterials.supplyRisk: Medium - Attrition 15-20% in relationship managers. Wage inflation 8-12%. Tech talent war with fintech.

overview.entryBarriers: Very High - (1) RBI banking license highly restrictive (only 2 new universal bank licenses in 10 years), (2) Minimum capital ₹500 Cr for small finance bank, ₹5,000+ Cr for universal bank, (3) Branch network takes decades to build (HDFC has 8,000+ branches), (4) Trust and brand critical - new entrants struggle, (5) Technology infrastructure expensive (₹1,000+ Cr investment), (6) Regulatory compliance burden (Basel III, AML, KYC, data privacy)
overview.regulatoryEnvironment: Heavily regulated by RBI. Key regulations: (1) CAR - Minimum 11.5% capital adequacy, (2) CRR/SLR - 4.5% + 18% of deposits in reserves/government securities, (3) NPA recognition - 90 days overdue must be classified as bad loan, (4) Priority Sector Lending - 40% of loans to agriculture, MSME, weaker sections, (5) Loan-to-Value limits on home/auto loans, (6) KYC/AML regulations strict, (7) Data localization - customer data must be stored in India, (8) Prompt Corrective Action for weak banks. Regulation increasing post-Yes Bank, PMC Bank crises.
overview.cyclicality: Moderate to High - Banking is pro-cyclical: (1) Credit growth accelerates in economic booms, slows in downturns, (2) NPAs spike during recessions (COVID saw NPA surge from 8% to 11% for system), (3) Net Interest Margins compressed when RBI cuts rates, expand when rates rise, (4) Fee income from markets/investments volatile. However, retail deposits provide stability. CASA balances sticky even in downturns.

threatOfNewEntrants.reasoning: RBI very restrictive with banking licenses. Only 2 universal bank licenses in decade. Payment banks/small finance banks allowed but can't compete with full-service banks. Capital requirements prohibitive. Incumbent branch networks create massive barriers.

bargainingPowerOfSuppliers.reasoning: Depositors have moderate power - can shift banks but switching costs exist (salary accounts, EMIs, relationships). CASA deposits give pricing power to banks. Fixed deposit rates competitive. Regulators (RBI) have high power - dictate rates, norms.

bargainingPowerOfBuyers.reasoning: Corporate borrowers negotiate aggressively - spreads compressed. Retail borrowers price-sensitive - home loan wars common. Credit cards churning high. However, switching costs exist (foreclosure charges, processing). Banks differentiate via service.

threatOfSubstitutes.reasoning: Multiple substitutes emerging: (1) NBFCs for loans (Bajaj Finance, LIC Housing), (2) Fintech lenders (Paytm, PhonePe), (3) Mutual funds for savings (SIPs), (4) Digital wallets (Paytm, PhonePe) replacing current accounts, (5) UPI direct bank transfers reducing credit card usage. However, full-service banking hard to replicate.

competitiveRivalry.reasoning: Intense competition: (1) Price wars in home loans (rates down to 8.4%), (2) Credit card cashback battles, (3) Zero-balance accounts, (4) Deposit rate competition, (5) Private banks vs PSU banks vs NBFCs vs fintech. Differentiation difficult - commoditized products.

emergingTrends: Digital-first banking - 90%+ transactions now digital. Branches becoming less relevant.
Embedded finance - Banking services within ecommerce/apps (Flipkart, Amazon Pay)
Account Aggregator framework enabling data portability - reduces stickiness
UPI credit line on tap - RBI proposal threatens credit card revenues
Neo-banks (Jupiter, Fi, NiYo) attracting young customers
AI/ML for credit underwriting - faster loan approvals, lower NPAs
Hyperlocal banking - WhatsApp banking, regional language interfaces
Climate finance - green loans, ESG-linked lending gaining traction
innovationsDisruptions: UPI replacing credit cards - 40% of card-like transactions now on UPI, zero fee
Digital lending platforms (Paytm, Amazon) threatening personal loan market
RBI's digital rupee (CBDC) could disintermediate banks in payments
Account Aggregator + Open Credit Enablement Network (OCEN) enables instant loans without banks
Buy-Now-Pay-Later (BNPL) from Simpl, LazyPay eating into credit card market
Challenger banks like Open, RazorpayX targeting MSME current accounts
